title: Universal SDK for WUSIWUG editors
body:
Lucia Browser SDK – Integration Spec (Wix / WordPress / Shopify / Framer)

🎯 Objective

Enable non-technical users (e.g. IG influencers, small merchants) to install Lucia SDK via a simple script embed, enabling automatic tracking of page views, button clicks, and scrolls.

🧩 Core Requirements

1. Universal Embed Script

  • Provide a single <script> tag to paste into Wix / WordPress / Shopify / Framer settings.

  • Example:

    

<script src="https://cdn.luciaprotocol.com/lucia-sdk-latest.min.js"></script>
<script>
  LuciaSDK.init({
    // API Key from https://ads.clickinsights.xyz
    api_key: 'bbbbbbbb-a7298818-3265d018-e9a648ae-95371b44-d25e6c77-38069b79-1e4bd263'
  });

  LuciaSDK.pageView('PAGE-NAME');
</script>

  • SDK auto-initializes via LuciaSDK.init()

2. Auto Page Tracking

  • SDK automatically triggers:

    LuciaSDK.pageView('projects')

  • Should debounce duplicate triggers (e.g., single-page app reloads or Wix internal router updates).

3. Button Click Tracking

  • Auto-attach click listeners to all visible <button> and <a> elements.

  • On click:

    LuciaSDK.buttonClick({
  ...
});

4. Scroll Tracking

  • Emit event when user scrolls past key thresholds (5%,10%,15%, 20%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%):

    LuciaSDK.trackScroll({ event: 'scroll', value: scrollDepth });

5. Attribution Context

Every event includes:

{
  "utm": getUtmParams(),
  "device": getBrowserData().device,
  "screen": getBrowserData().screen,
  "browser": getBrowserData().browser
}

6. CMS-Specific Implementation Notes

Platform | Implementation Path | Notes -- | -- | -- Wix | Paste JS into Site Settings → Tracking & Analytics (Runs on all pages). | Fallback: per-page embed if global not available. WordPress | Add script via Header/Footer plugin or functions.php. | Compatible with both Classic & Gutenberg editors. Shopify | Add to theme.liquid before . | Use window.ShopifyAnalytics.meta to enrich attribution data. Framer | Paste via Custom Code → Footer Script. | SPA-friendly — detect route changes via window.onpopstate.

User Story 📸

As an IG influencer with a Wix site,
I want to install Lucia SDK by pasting one script,
So I can see which buttons and pages my visitors interact with
— without needing to code or configure tracking manually.

User Story ⛴️

As an e-commerce store owner with a Shopify site, among other sites like Amazon
I want to install Lucia SDK by pasting one script,
So I can track conversion rate on major buttons for purchasing, track conversion rates on other minor buttons like contact form fill outs, and pages my visitors interact with
— without needing to code extensively or configure tracking page by page.

✅ Deliverables

  • CDN-ready build sdk.min.js

  • Cross-platform event tracking (page, click, scroll)

  • Docs examples for Wix, WordPress, Shopify, Framer

  • Event schema alignment with backend fingerprints table

title: Passively Collect All Emails
body:
# Detect “email-ish” fields with a scored heuristic + runtime signals. 

input[type=email]

input[type=text|search], textarea whose name/id/class include: email, e-mail, mail, login, user, username, handle, contact, work_email, business_email

autocomplete hints: email, username

Associated <label for=...> or ARIA text containing: email, e-mail, work email, business email, school email, username, handle

pattern attributes that include @ or typical email regex fragments

placeholder text with @ or “email”, “username”

### Steps Summarized

1. Detect likely email fields (reuse your detector).
2. Attach listeners (change, blur, submit, paste).
3. Validate & normalize the value (RFC-5322-ish regex + trim/lowercase).
4. Respect consent/config (don’t capture if disallowed).
5. Queue and send to API backend

# Scoring

Score each field with additive weighted rules to produce a confidence score (0–1).

+5 if type=email

+4 if autocomplete=email

+3 if name|id|class matches /\b(e-?mail|mail)\b/i

+2 if label/placeholder contains “email” or includes @

+1 if pattern contains @ or TLDs (.com, .edu, etc.)

−2 if label contains just “username” (track separately as username-ish)

−3 if field is hidden/readonly/offscreen

# False Positive Control

Keep email vs username as separate labels. Some apps accept both in one box; that’s fine—expose both labels with confidence.

title: Agentic exploration
body:
I’d like to propose enhancing the /ask plugin with agent-like capabilities, allowing the LLM to explore the repository, organization, or external resources as needed to answer a question more effectively.

For example, currently the LLM only has access to documents explicitly attached in the triggering comment. However, relevant information often exists elsewhere (such as in related issues/PRs, README, code files) that the LLM cannot currently access.

By enabling the LLM to explore more freely, we could greatly improve the quality and context-awareness of its responses.

The LLM agent could:
-	Access repo files: Fetch any file in the repo (e.g. source code, README, config files) to gain context.
-	Follow cross-references: Detect and fetch linked issues or PRs (e.g. `in #100 this was already implemented`) and retrieve their body or summarized comments.
-	Leverage external resources: Fetch linked documents or URLs from comments to extract additional context.
-	Explore related content: Automatically detect and pull in other relevant comments in the same issue that contain documents or links.

Use cases
-	When answering a question about a plugin, the LLM could fetch the README or main code file to better understand its purpose.
-	If someone references another issue (see #123), the LLM could fetch its description or comments to provide context.
-	If earlier comments contain documents or links, the LLM could use them even if they weren’t in the triggering comment.

Benefits
-	More complete and contextual answers
-	Reduced need for users to repeat or restate context
-	Smarter, more autonomous behavior aligned with how developers actually communicate

title: Incentives for lower estimates
body:
This is a draft/discussion and needs more research and ideas. 

As the DAO grows and trust naturally decreases, there should be an incentive for the author and possibly assignee for them to want to make time estimates for tasks as short as realistically possible (or just as close to accurate as possible)

Otherwise the natural incentive is to over estimate all tasks to make pricing larger than it should be. 

Currently the only soft incentive is the financier/admins/managers needing to approve a time estimate before "funding" tasks. 

#### time accuracy 

This is very tricky to do because even if it's a `<4 hour` task, in our system design it's perfectly acceptable to spread that project over a few days. Because of this, we can't simply check the timeline timestamps of when they started and when they pushed their last commit or comment to determine how long it actually took them. Finding accurate time estimates may be better suited for an LLM to be the judge but even still it might be tough to do with accuracy. RAG with all the comments in the repository may provide more clues/hints for accuracy but this still seems shaky. We could do an experiment with estimating time estimates based on RAG context and then we could consider a penalty/bonus depending on how aligned the estimate is for the LLM compared to who set the label?

It could even be revealed only when the project is closed as complete which may incentivize further the label setter to tell the truth. I.e. "accurate label set bonus reward" which can be a gradient based on how close in milliseconds the label value is to the LLM estimate.

title: Set labels command
body:
In an effort to reduce the distinction between "contributors" and "collaborators" we should let anybody whitelisted to set labels. 

It may not be user friendly unless we do a mini "search" and select the highest match. For example:

/labels 15 min high priority 

Then we need to intelligently pick the correct labels (or create new ones in the case of the time estimates)  

We also need an admin control (maybe simpler to write in the config yml?) to let specific users set a class of labels: time, priority, price.

```yml
time:
   users:
      - 0x4007
      - whilefoo
priority: 
   users:
      - keyrxng 
price:
   users: 
      - rndquu 
```

This can be really nice with the natural mini LLM command router. Particularly in outside of GitHub contexts like telegram or a google docs comment: 

> @Ubiquityos set to 15 min high priority task 

### v2 vision

The reason why I want to blur the lines between collaborator/contributor is because I think the concept of a "core team" is not truly DAO like. Eventually we should allow for label classes to be editable based on XP. For example:

```yml
time:
   xp: 0
priority: 
   xp: 1000
price:
   xp: 2000
```

And ideally with a time decay element so that you need to be actively contributing to have the privilege to set these. And of course, collaborators would be able to set using the GitHub UI. 
   

> I can't seem to set labels. 15 minutes sounds about right

title: Create a base reward for users within this plugin
body:
Yes I do agree that we should be able to chain these results. It's just that this plugin never had the capability to actually attribute rewards to a user, this was the responsibility of `conversation-rewards`. We could think of splitting this and have the logic here later on.

_Originally posted by @gentlementlegen in https://github.com/ubiquibot/assistive-pricing/issues/27#issuecomment-2338184070_

This plugin should output a `Reward` compatible output to be able to be chained with other plugins. The current shape of the rewards is as follows:
https://github.com/ubiquibot/conversation-rewards?tab=readme-ov-file#data-structure

What this plugin should output is the following section:
```json
"userName": {
  "task": {
    "reward": 37.5,
    "multiplier": 1
  }
}
```
which corresponds to the current reward within the label divided by the amount of users assigned to the task. Once in the output, chained plugins could pick this up and use it for further calculations.

title: Include PR context
body:
> Could you can try running `/rewrite` on https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/command-start-stop/issues/154 please?

> It meets all the criteria for it, and would be great to see it in action. (My concern is that it does not factor in the PR or any code at all, which is a huge amount of missing context as it's unreasonable to expect all technical details to be mentioned in the conversation but their acceptance by reviewers is specification discovery in and of itself)


As a contributor who over the years has often taken on ambiguous specs and saw them through to completion, it has been my experience that, oftentimes a lot of ambiguous specs shift product-design and engineering effort onto contributors and it often goes unrecognized the amount of work that gets put in.

For this plugin to work as best as it can it must also have context of the active PR because, in my experience, a lot of what I implement gets accepted without it being mentioned in the spec/issue convo. It only appears in conversation through review if it needs tweaked or rejected so this means:

- anything off-spec which is undiscussed (an inherent acceptance of that deliverable) is unknown to the llm
- it does not surface reviews or PR comments

Finally, it's unreasonable to expect (I think) contributors who have done the extra product-design and engineering work to list all tech specs of their implementation into the issue, before then calling `/rewrite`. Better UX if the plugin/agent handles this themselves.

One recent example (although there are tens more I could think of):

- https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/command-start-stop/issues/154 - barren, very little activity, no discussion around my suggestions which have all been accepted at this point
- https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/command-start-stop/pull/185 - context rich, laden with activity, huge amount of undiscussed technical details that have been accepted, plenty of changes requested by reviewers

^ All the llm gets to see is an empty conversation spanning back to March, then my impl details being ignored (so it likely won't (or shouldn't) accept them as new spec details), while the PR continues to move.

title: Make text-conversation-rewards MCP Compliant
body:
To enable broader interoperability, we should make the `text-conversation-rewards` plugin MCP compliant. This would allow any LLM or external system to interact with it using the Model Context Protocol, rather than relying on UbiquityOS-specific conventions.

### Key Points & Discussion

- **MCP Integration:**  
  Use the [typescript-sdk](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/typescript-sdk) for standard MCP integration. This may require changes to the `manifest.json` schema or an adapter for compatibility.

- **Deployment:**  
  All plugins should have a Deno Deploy endpoint. For plugins currently running as GitHub Actions, the Deno endpoint should trigger the Action (see [daemon-pricing](https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/daemon-pricing) for an example).

- **Streamable HTTP:**  
  MCP works through Streamable HTTP. Deno supports streaming, but it's not strictly required for all MCPs.

- **Context Handling:**  
  The main challenge is providing the necessary context (e.g., repo/issue/task info) when running outside GitHub (e.g., Telegram, CLI). Platform adapters may be needed to capture and provide this context.

- **Platform Agnosticism:**  
  `text-conversation-rewards` can be made platform-agnostic if we decouple content retrieval from GitHub and allow input from other sources (e.g., Google Docs, Telegram). The main non-agnostic part is currently content retrieval.

- **Standardization:**  
  Standardizing Deno deploy templates (similar to Cloudflare) will help. We have implemented some MCP-like standards already, but aligning with industry standards is beneficial.

- **Next Steps:**  
  - Prototype MCP compliance with `text-conversation-rewards` first.
  - Consider making a special AI system capability for plugins that use the current manifest format.
  - If successful, expand to other plugins as needed.

---

**Transferred from:** [ubiquity-os/plugins-wishlist#73](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugins-wishlist/issues/73)

title: `/finish` command won't work if it was triggered with LLM router like `@UbiquityOS can you generate rewards`
body:
I realized this won't work if it was triggered with LLM router like `@UbiquityOS can you generate rewards` but currently there's no easy fix for this so I'm just leaving this comment to acknowledge this issue

_Originally posted by @whilefoo in https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/text-conversation-rewards/pull/343#discussion_r2080353080_

# Command routing via `@UbiquityOS` mention is not handled correctly

## What happened

Commands triggered via LLM routing (e.g., mentioning `@UbiquityOS` with a prompt like `@UbiquityOS can you generate rewards`) are not correctly processed by the system. This is due to current limitations in how mentions and command parsing are handled (we chack for bot events, triggerd by user events).

## What was expected

Commands issued through mentions (such as `@UbiquityOS can you generate rewards`) should be routed and handled in the same way as directly issued commands, and understand that this was triggered through the LLM in the kernel

## Reproduction steps

1. Mention the bot in a comment or message with a natural-language request (e.g., `@UbiquityOS can you generate rewards`).
2. Observe that the command is not executed correctly.
3. Compare this to behavior when the same command is issued without using the LLM call.

## Notes

Currently, there is no simple fix for this limitation. This issue exists to acknowledge the problem and track potential improvements in command parsing or mention handling in the future.

title: Update configuration action
body:
The `update-configuration.yml` doesn't apply formatting according to the prettier configuration

Also I think it would be better to make this a [custom action](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/sharing-automations/creating-actions/about-custom-actions) which would prevent duplication, we can use Typescript instead of writing code in the YML and allow us to change it without having to apply changes to every repo that forks plugin-template.

The only question is how to specify where the schema is located in the code, it could be something like this:
```yml
- name: update configuration
  uses: ubiquity-os/update-configuration
  with:
    filepath: './src/types'
    typeboxSchemaName: 'pluginSettingsSchema'
```
and then we can dynamically import it!

In the future we could also support different schema libraries (zod, joi...)

title: Re-open PR if re-assigned by admin
body:
Perhaps this is redundant but I've seen it a handful of times within the last few days.

- contributor submits PR
- user is ejected for deadlines or similar
- admin(s) decide to re-assign the contributor
- PR remains `closed` or it's re-opened and is stale due to commits pushed while `closed`
- in [this instance](https://github.com/ubiquity/stake.ubq.fi/pull/10) I'd expect to see the PR update automatically with the commits that the contributor pushed while `closed` (as is the case with [my own](https://github.com/ubiquity-os-marketplace/command-start-stop/pull/185) I suspect)

---

1. If an admin re-assigns the contributor, automatically re-open their most recent PR.
2. I'm unsure (able to test it with my own PR #185 when it gets re-opened) if pushing a single new commit after it's re-opened will update all missing commits. However, if it does, then we should consider automating this too.

---

Outcomes:

1. We improve reviewer QOL. They re-assign and don't have to deal with telling the user they need to push a fresh commit or open a new PR.
2. We improve DX by automating the process of pulling in the most recent commits from that branch without them needing to force a commit just to refresh the PR for reviewers.

title: VSCode Extension
body:
We should consider developing a VSCode extension for work.ubq.fi (or any platform for viewing issues with partners) to increase user engagement. While there are many logins on the platform, they aren't converting into active contributors working on tasks. By creating a VSCode extension, we can provide a more seamless and accessible way for developers to interact with tasks directly within their development environment, potentially increasing contributions and task completions.

Possible Extensions:
1. One-Click Issue Setup: Allow developers to easily set up and sync issues from work.ubq.fi directly in VSCode with minimal setup.
2. Task Management: View and update task statuses directly from VSCode.
3. Notifications: Get real-time updates on task changes, comments, or deadlines directly in the IDE.

I think increasing coupling between this plugin and this UI is fine because otherwise it is not possible for external DAO contributors to work on tasks. I can't think of another way around this. 

1. Check if they are a collaborator of the org
2. If they are not, we need to parse the settings, possibly by exposing an API[^1^]

title: Staking v2 audit
body:
Depends on https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/issues/985

We must audit the newly created staking contracts.

Scope:
1. https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/ac517749fdddef3b9755c305224e2aacf14b7756/packages/contracts/src/dollar/facets/StakingFacet.sol
2. https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/ac517749fdddef3b9755c305224e2aacf14b7756/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibStaking.sol

Staking use case example for an end user:
1. User [stakes](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/ac517749fdddef3b9755c305224e2aacf14b7756/packages/contracts/src/dollar/facets/StakingFacet.sol#L64) `LUSD/UUSD LP` tokens from [Curve pool](https://etherscan.io/address/0xcc68509f9ca0e1ed119eac7c468ec1b1c42f384f) 
2. Some time passes
3. User [unstakes](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/ac517749fdddef3b9755c305224e2aacf14b7756/packages/contracts/src/dollar/facets/StakingFacet.sol#L69) position and collects [UBQ](https://etherscan.io/address/0x4e38D89362f7e5db0096CE44ebD021c3962aA9a0) rewards

Notes:
- Staking code is based on [MasterChef](https://github.com/sushi-labs/sushiswap/blob/271458b558afa6fdfd3e46b8eef5ee6618b60f9d/contracts/MasterChef.sol)
- Auditor should consider how the staking feature works in context of the diamond pattern and [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/ac517749fdddef3b9755c305224e2aacf14b7756/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol) where users are able to:
  a) send [LUSD](https://etherscan.io/address/0x5f98805a4e8be255a32880fdec7f6728c6568ba0) and [UBQ](https://etherscan.io/address/0x4e38D89362f7e5db0096CE44ebD021c3962aA9a0) in order to mint [UUSD](https://etherscan.io/address/0xb6919Ef2ee4aFC163BC954C5678e2BB570c2D103)
  b) redeem [UUSD](https://etherscan.io/address/0xb6919Ef2ee4aFC163BC954C5678e2BB570c2D103) in order to get [LUSD](https://etherscan.io/address/0x5f98805a4e8be255a32880fdec7f6728c6568ba0) and [UBQ](https://etherscan.io/address/0x4e38D89362f7e5db0096CE44ebD021c3962aA9a0)

I suppose staked token must never be the same one used as collateral in [LibUbiquityPool](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/ac517749fdddef3b9755c305224e2aacf14b7756/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol) because it may skew calculations [here](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/ac517749fdddef3b9755c305224e2aacf14b7756/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol#L354) and [here](https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/blob/ac517749fdddef3b9755c305224e2aacf14b7756/packages/contracts/src/dollar/libraries/LibUbiquityPool.sol#L717).

title: Adding collateral enabled checks for redemption collections might make users uncapable of redeming their funds.
body:
[MEDIUM] - Adding collateral enabled checks for redemption collections might make users uncapable of redeming their funds.

This PR is intended to fix [the bug described in this Sherlock issue](https://github.com/sherlock-audit/2023-12-ubiquity-judging/issues/29). However, the Sherlock report is actually wrong and the vulnerability described must not be considered as an issue in the first place, so the collateralEnabled check should not be added on collecting redemptions.

Enabling/disabling collaterals allows the protocol to add or remove supported collaterals, not to be used under uncertain situations, such as a hack. In such type of situations, the `isRedeemPaused` check can be enabled to prevent users from redeeming if something wrong is currently  happening.

The problem with adding the `collateralEnabled` check here is that if Ubiquity decides to disable a collateral in the future, some redemptions might be already queued, so users won't be able to withdraw their funds.


Recommendation: Remove the `collateralEnabled` check for collecting redemptions.

_Originally posted by @0xadrii in https://github.com/ubiquity/ubiquity-dollar/pull/894#discussion_r1572571068_

title: Octokit helper methods
body:
We are using two GraphQL queries for fetching linked issues and fetching linked PRs so the code gets duplicated in each plugin that needs it. 

I think this is helpful for a couple of reasons:
- No duplication of code
- Less bugs because currently each repo have its own implementation (daemon-pull-reviewer's implementation was typed wrongly causing a bug)
- New contributors can use these helper methods without having to worry about implementation

Octokit supports plugins which essentially add methods to the Octokit object. 
Example usage:
```js
octokit.helpers.getLinkedIssues({
  owner,
  repo,
  prNumber
})
octokit.helpers.getLinkedPullRequests({
  owner,
  repo,
  issueNumber
})
```

title: Plugin "Classes"
body:
It's slowly becoming clear that we have different categories of plugins that interoperate. Perhaps its worth formalizing the system architecture to support this concept. 

To clarify, the kernel should be a generalized webhook quarterback, capable of relaying any webhook to any plugin, based on the config of the organization. It should not be tied to GitHub only. 

Plugin Classes:

- Financial Incentives[^1^]
- Vector Embeddings[^2^]
- Model Context Protocol[^3^]

[^1^]: Currently known as `text-conversation-rewards` and with its "sub-plugins" these should always return a "reward request" which implies that it will give a financial reward to the user for doing something. 
[^2^]: We already have several "sub-plugins" that rely on a single vector embeddings database, such as `/ask`, `deduplication`, `/annotate` and `task-matchmaking`. 
[^3^]: This isn't clear to me if we should apply this on the kernel directly and/or on the `command-` and `daemon-` plugins OR if we should make another "Plugin Class" that has "sub-plugins" which are "MCP enabled".

title: Enhance Kernel with Generalized Request Handling & Secure External API
body:
#### **Summary**  
Expand the kernel's ingress beyond GitHub webhooks by introducing a secure public API endpoint. This will allow external services to trigger GitHub Actions workflows, effectively turning Actions into a general-purpose backend.  

#### **Key Features**  
- **Public API Endpoint**:  
  - Accepts external requests to execute plugins via the kernel.  
  - Supports dynamic execution of actions, enabling broader integrations.  

- **Authentication & Security**:  
  - Requests must include:  
    - `installation ID` (this allows us to target the correct org)  
    - `repository ID` (optionally, to load a specific repo config)  
  - Partners must provide an **encrypted password** stored in their config.  
    - Uses **X25519 encryption** (same system as EVM private key storage).  
    - Clients pass a password in the request header, which the kernel decrypts.  
  - Rate-limiting to prevent abuse.  

- **Execution Flow**:  
  1. Kernel receives an authenticated request with at least `installation ID` and `password`.  
  2. It decrypts the password and verifies the request.  
  3. Executes the specified plugin and returns the response.  

- **Webhook Replies**:  
  - Requests can include a `response URL` header.  
  - The kernel posts the result from the action plugin to this URL upon completion.  

- **Future Enhancements**:  
  - Investigate **cross-plugin communication** via the kernel relay.  

#### **Next Steps**  
- Define API Contract (Headers, Payload Format)
- Implement Authentication & Decryption Logic
- Extend Kernel Request Handling to Invoke Plugins
- Add Support for Webhook Response Delivery

title: Improve Plugin Callback to Kernel
body:
Right now, each plugin posts comments on its own using Octokit. We should change this so the kernel posts comments for all the plugins. If we plan to do something like [this issue](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugins-wishlist/issues/54#issuecomment-2652784793), this change will be important.

Instead of each plugin creating a comment on its own, the plugin SDK should send the data to the kernel, and the kernel will handle creating the event.

title: Add support for calling worker plugins from URL set in the manifest
body:
Depends on https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugin-template/issues/33

[This](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugin-template/issues/33) task introduces a new property `homepage_url` (which is a plugin worker URL) in the [manifest.json](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugin-template/blob/8d8f06958ca1273f299ae0bc2a51a6c512411af9/manifest.json) file.  

The idea is that now the bot's config will contain only `org/repo` in the plugin's config. For example [here](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/.ubiquity-os/blob/67df7f5643edcaa3899304481f7ff4ef74a1041a/.github/.ubiquity-os.config.dev.yml#L3) instead of
```
- plugin: https://ubiquity-os-comment-vector-embeddings-development.ubiquity.workers.dev
```
we will set `org/repo` value:
```
- plugin: ubiquity-os-marketplace/text-vector-embeddings
```

The kernel should support calling a worker URL if it is set in the [manifest.json](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugin-template/blob/8d8f06958ca1273f299ae0bc2a51a6c512411af9/manifest.json).

How it is supposed to work:
1. If `plugin` config property contains URL then call it as a worker (this is to support close sourced plugins + for backwards compatibility)
2. If `plugin` config property contains `org/repo` value then fetch plugin's [manifest.json](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugin-template/blob/8d8f06958ca1273f299ae0bc2a51a6c512411af9/manifest.json):
  - If `homepage_url` property is present in the manifest then call the plugin as a worker
  - If `homepage_url` property is missing then call plugin as a github action

Original [comment](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/plugin-template/issues/33#issuecomment-2549092026)

title: Hitting rate limits on too many webhook requests
body:
There might be cases when the kernel receives too many webhook events, for example:
- [some](https://t.me/UbiquityDAO/29891/32505) file is deleted from all of the repos
- too many partners are using the `ubiquity-os` app

When the kernel receives too many webhooks it exceeds github API rate limits which causes the kernel to be unresponsive and throwing the `Rate limit hit with \"GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{path}\", retrying in 1469 seconds` error. 

There should not be any downtimes in the kernel work.

title: Abstract Kernel and Plugin Setup into UI
body: Not sure if this fits here or in [ubiquity-os-kernel](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/ubiquity-os-kernel/). As soon as the [CLI tooling to setup kernel](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/ubiquity-os-kernel/issues/171) is merged, we can take our first shot into abstracting the entire kernel and plugin setup into an UI, at least the worker ones.  By authenticating with Cloudflare and GitHub we should be able to not only deploy the workers (kernel and plugins), but setup the repos for the org as well. title: SDK improvements
body:
The SDK could get some improvements to make it more friendly to use

- `"hono"` doesn't seem to be shipped with it but is required to serve the plugin, so it might be included, or even better, encapsulated within the SDK.
- there is `createPlugin` and `createActionPlugin`. Maybe we could have only `createPlugin`, that would change behavior depending on either the runtime or the environment (e.g. running on a Worker / Action). What would be great that the user doesn't have to worry about this, and locally a GitHub plugin would run as a server, this way it would be easy to debug locally.
- the `Manifest` type is not exported. The main issue is that when a JSON is imported directly through `import`, the type of `ubiquity:listeners` is a `string[]` and is incompatible with `Manifest`.
- now both Actions and Workers come with a manifest, and this doesn't seem to be currently available for `createActionPlugin` (might be solved if we merge both function).
- the validate and decode + inputs could be handled in the SDK (currently just Decodes with no error nor Default).
- expose the `postComment` methos to make sure we have metadata on the comments
- remove duplicated declaration of `postComment`
- improve logs for error (there is none at the moment)
- add GraphQL paginate to Octokit
- option to disable the signature check for ease of use locally

title: Decrypt sensitive config parameters
body:
Right now there are 2 ways to hide sensitive bot's config parameters:
1. Make a config repository private (organization collaborators can still see the values though)
2. Fork plugin, self host and set sensitive parameters in env variables (bad UX for partners)

Sensitive config parameters could be encrypted via our own `x25519_PUBLIC_KEY` (the same one we use for encrypting partners' private keys) and the kernel could then decrypt them.

So as a part of this issue the kernel should be able to decrypt config parameters on initial config parsing. 

The expected flow for parsing a single config param:
1. Try to decrypt the parameter. 
2. If it is decrypted then use the decrypted parameter.
3. If there's a decryption error then assume the parameter is not encrypted and use a raw value.

Notice that there is a difference between decrypting: 
1. Unencrypted param
2. Encrypted param with another PK

I hope https://doc.libsodium.org/ distinguishes those 2 errors because in the 2nd case it will be a very subtle bug and the kernel should ideally throw an error like `The config parameter encryption is invalid` so that partner knew he did something wrong on encrypting a param.

P.S. Handy tool: https://keygen.ubq.fi/

title: Ubiquity Credit
body:
I think we have a shot at making the crypto industry's first under collateralized credit program. 

Normally you need to be over collateralized to borrow, but, we have two key points of leverage in our infrastructure that can make under collateralized credit work. 

1. Contributor reputation: we can track a contributor's earnings over time and extend credit based on their monthly average earnings. For example, a user earns on average $5000 a month for the last three months, so they can borrow $5000 from us at X% APR if they need liquidity. Not sure what a good minimum threshold is but obviously the shorter the riskier. We could start a pilot with a whitelist of OG contributors first. We can consider only allowing card minting to disincentivize the use of leverage/trading with the funds. 

2. Permits: wages are distributed through our system and could be used for automatic payback (or garnishing wages) which could help make financing easier to payback. 

I think the biggest piece of advantage is the reputation system though. Contributors who have old GitHub profiles can be more inclined to pay back debts to continue using the system if they have an old GitHub profile and worked for a long time in the system.

title: Allow Users to Select and Configure Specific Plugin Branches
body:
Currently, the plugin installer GUI assumes users always want to configure and install the version of a plugin found on a single, predefined branch (initially hardcoded to `development`, later updated to the repository's default branch). However, plugins often have different versions, features, or configuration schemas across various branches (e.g., `main`, `development`, feature branches, release tags).

Users need the ability to explicitly select the desired branch for a plugin they are configuring, and the system must ensure that the correct manifest is used for rendering the UI and the correct identifier (Action reference or URL) is saved in the configuration file.

**Desired Behavior / Acceptance Criteria:**

1.  The configuration editor should display a dropdown allowing users to select from the available branches of the plugin's source repository.
2.  When editing an existing plugin configuration, the dropdown should pre-select the branch specified in the saved configuration identifier (`owner/repo@branch` or parsed from URL).
3.  When editing an existing plugin configuration, the editor UI should initially render based on the manifest fetched from the *configured* branch.
4.  When the user selects a different branch from the dropdown, the editor UI should dynamically update by fetching and rendering the manifest from the newly selected branch.
5.  When saving the configuration, the `plugin` identifier stored in the `uses` array must accurately reflect the user's selected branch:
    *   For Action-based plugins: `owner/repo@selectedBranch`
    *   For URL-based plugins: The correctly transformed URL corresponding to the `selectedBranch` (e.g., `https://...-selected-branch-suffix.domain.com`).
6.  UI elements should be consistent with the rest of the application.
7.  Informational/success toast notifications should auto-dismiss.

## Main issues

We will have problems saving different branches because it is technically correct to have two similar plugins pointing to two different branches. Similarly the plugin would not know we want to change the target branch but will instead stack plugins from different branches together.

title: Set `organization/repository` names in the plugins' configs
body:
Depends on https://github.com/ubiquity-os/ubiquity-os-kernel/issues/224

Plugin installer should always set `organization/repository` value in the config's [plugin](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/.ubiquity-os/blob/67df7f5643edcaa3899304481f7ff4ef74a1041a/.github/.ubiquity-os.config.dev.yml#L3) property for all of the plugins.

For example [here](https://github.com/ubiquity-os/.ubiquity-os/blob/67df7f5643edcaa3899304481f7ff4ef74a1041a/.github/.ubiquity-os.config.dev.yml#L3) instead of
```
- plugin: https://ubiquity-os-comment-vector-embeddings-development.ubiquity.workers.dev
```
plugin installer UI should set `org/repo` value:
```
- plugin: ubiquity-os-marketplace/text-vector-embeddings
```

title: Save EOA to a DB
body:
Depends on https://github.com/ubiquity/safe.ubq.fi/pull/2

Right now `safe.ubq.fi` is able to generate EOA address from a passkey but doesn't save it to a database.

This way user has to perform an additional registration step:
1. User calls `/register` and the bot replies with "Pls create an EOA at safe.ubq.fi"
2. User creates a new EOA at `safe.ubq.fi`
3. User calls `/wallet` to register his address in our DB (additional step)

We should support the following flow:
1. User calls `/register` and the bot replies with "Pls create an EOA at safe.ubq.fi"
2. User creates a new EOA at `safe.ubq.fi` **and** `safe.ubq.fi`'s backend saves user's address to a DB

Ideally for a DB we should use a partner's json storage (i.e. his `ubiquibot-config` repository) but if https://github.com/ubiquibot/plugin-template/issues/2 is not implemented at the time somebody starts this issue then we can simply use our supabase instance and leave "json storage" for the next feature iteration.

Original [comment](https://github.com/ubiquibot/plugins-wishlist/issues/35#issuecomment-2292625984).

title: Stop the workflow within a reasonable amount of time
body: If the contributor does not have all of the required env vars set up for the workflow to successfully deploy then the workflow will remaining running for the entire 6hrs, as can be seen [here](https://github.com/ubq-testing/automated-merging/actions/workflows/deno-deploy.yml) This is redundant to allow it to keep running and it eats through their monthly quota of non-billable minutes - https://github.com/ubq-testing/automated-merging/actions/workflows/deno-deploy.yml - many timeouts and two recent success after I set up my details.

title: V1: Backfill and validate historical permit payouts (GitHub + on-chain)
body:
## Goals

- Recover historical permit data from GitHub issues and comments.
- Rebuild permits into the current schema and de-duplicate against the database.
- Validate remaining permits against on-chain activity on Ethereum and Gnosis.
- Produce a final report of matched, withdrawn, and unresolved permits.
- Improve the foundation of the XP system
- have near complete auditability with 1:1 ratio of `issue label totals == stored permit totals`, or at the very least, each task having verified/tracked payouts

***

## Scope

### 1. Scrape and reconstruct permits

- Enumerate all Ubiquity-owned GitHub orgs and repositories.
- For each issue:
    - Filter comments created by the `prod` & `dev` UbiquityOS.
    - Extract any base64-encoded permit URLs from comment bodies.
    - Decode the base64 payloads and rebuild permit objects into the current expected shape.
- Exclude any permits that already exist in the production database.


### 2. Ad-hoc / manual permits

- In addition to bot comments, include comments by:
    - `@0x4007` for manually sent claim URLs when the bot was down or broken (personally happened to me).
    - `@gentlementlegen` where claim URLs may have been copy-pasted manually when things were down/broken (I'm positive I saw it happen for another contributor, if not myself).
- Parse and process these comments for claim URLs using the same pipeline as bot comments.
- Treat these as best-effort coverage to improve audit completeness.


### 3. Permit validity checks

- For all reconstructed permits not already in the DB:
    - Check on-chain whether the permit nonce has been invalidated.
        - Case A: The permit was claimed by the `beneficiary`.
        - Case B: The signer invalidated/withdrew the permit via the `owner` address.
- If claimed:
    - Match the permit directly to an on-chain transaction involving the `beneficiary`.
- If not claimed:
    - Attempt to locate the invalidation transaction from the `owner` address; otherwise classify as “assumed withdrawn”.

***

## On-chain validation (Ethereum + Gnosis)

Both options require mapping `beneficiary` addresses to users in the production DB.

### Common requirement

- Map `beneficiary` → user by:
    - Using the current wallet mapping in the production DB where possible.
    - Where mappings have changed, parse the `username` from the original bot permit comment to resolve historical ownership.


### Option A: CSV-based, thresholded

- Group reconstructed permits by `beneficiary` address and sum total amounts.
- Exclude addresses with a total under a configurable threshold (e.g. 1,000 USD equivalent).
- For remaining addresses:
    - Manually export transaction history CSVs from Etherscan and GnosisScan.
    - Parse CSVs and group transfers that match `permit transfer from` patterns from known funding wallets.
- Accept that low-earning contributors below the threshold may remain unresolved.


### Option B: Programmatic, full coverage

- Build a structure such as `Record<IssueUrl, Array<{ ...Permit, txHash }>>`.
- For each distinct wallet address:
    - Page through full transaction history from RPC / explorer APIs.
    - Attempt to match permit parameters to on-chain call arguments or transfer patterns.
- For permits without an associated transaction hash:
    - Group them by contributor and issue.
    - Enforce that each contributor can only successfully claim once per issue.
    - Write leftover unmatched permits to a file for manual inspection.
- Store matched permits (with tx hash) keyed by issue/repo/org.

***

## Scale / complexity

- Expected scale: thousands of transactions and hundreds of missing permits.
- Non-trivial data volume and debugging effort across:
    - GitHub API scraping and parsing.
    - Base64 decoding and schema normalization.
    - On-chain history queries on two networks.
    - Heuristic matching and manual review for edge cases.

title: Copilot assignment via the Github API
body:
**Putting myself out of a job here, but here we go 😅**

[As of yesterday](https://github.blog/changelog/2025-12-03-assign-issues-to-copilot-using-the-api/), it’s now possible to assign **Copilot** to issues via the REST and GraphQL APIs.

This opens up some interesting opportunities we could explore:

- A plugin or kernel extension that directly bridges the kernel with Copilot.
- Automated weekly or monthly **refactoring** tasks planned and executed by Copilot — either within a parent issue with a few sub-issues, or as a single “spec → plan → execute” workflow.
- Automated **documentation updates** across all plugins and projects on a set schedule.

If some of these don’t fit as new plugins, they could still be packaged as reusable workflows. I’m sure there are more ideas along these lines, but these are a few clear starting points.

title: "Savings Account"
body: A thought that occurred to me is if we make a "savings account" deposit button, we can simulate a banking experience. Proposed yield mechanic: Uniswap v4 concentrated liquidity at `0.9999 - 1.0001` I'm unsure how accurate the projection is, but anything above 5% is marketable as a "savings account" I think! > > > Given your screenshot data: > • Curve pool daily volume ≈ $27.9 k > • TVL ≈ $93.5 k > • Fee ≈ 0.04 % > → Fee yield ≈ 0.012 % per day ≈ 4.4 % APR before CRV boosts (close to the shown 2–3 %). > > Now suppose you moved that volume onto a Uniswap-style narrow-range LP: > 1. Range width = 0.02 % (0.9999–1.0001) > → ≈ 5 000 × capital efficiency vs full-range. > 2. Only a fraction of swaps cross your ticks — say 20–40 % in range. > 3. Fee tier = 0.01 %. > > Approximate effective boost: > > 4.4\% \times \frac{0.01}{0.04} \times 5000 \times 0.3 \approx 165\% \text{ APR} > > If the peg drifts slightly less, uptime rises, so ~100–300 % APR is plausible; if it drifts more, you fall out of range and it collapses back to single-digit yield. > > So: > > Expect maybe ~50 ×–70 × yield boost vs Curve, only while in-range. > Out-of-range = 0 fees; you’ll need automation or active rebalancing to realize the upper bound. Of course, it is implied that we would abstract all the operations away from the user i.e. swapping half the value into the other asset, depositing the liquidity etc.

The goal is to provide simple, useful statistics such as:

- Exportable stats and visualizations
- Time-of-day and day-of-week breakdowns

This will help teams understand when contributors are most active and adjust workflows for better efficiency (for example our reminders)

## Blockers
- **Timeline and analytics views are currently blocked.**
- The root cause is that artifact data does not include enriched XP event metadata (timestamp, node_id, and precise URL for each XP event).
- The current artifact only points to the parent issue, not the specific comment/PR/etc.
- Fetching this data at runtime is not feasible due to the volume of fetches and the IndexedDB-backed instant analytics requirement.
- The required solution is to enrich artifact data at build time (using the GitHub API) to include all necessary metadata for each XP event.

title: Codebase semantic search
body:
For on boarding new developers, or just to synchronize existing developers, we should be generating embeddings of our code base. We can do per file or on the function level, depending on which has better performance.

I asked GPT for the best sorting method on the [embedding leaderboard](https://huggingface.co/spaces/mteb/leaderboard). 

Later it clarified that we should be sorting by "retrieval with instructions" for best results. 

[FollowIR-7B](https://huggingface.co/jhu-clsp/FollowIR-7B) is the highest ranked. 

We should be doing this in two steps:
1. Primarily, we should be generating embeddings of the new files when pull requests are merged.
2. We should have a tool that we can run manually (manual dispatch on GitHub actions ok) to generate all the embeddings. This would be expected to be used if there were pushes directly to the default branch and were not added via pull request

### additional context

> For this use case (codebase question-answering), your focus should be on retrieval tasks. Based on the criteria provided, here’s how I would prioritize the metrics:

> 1. **Retrieval Average (15 datasets)**: This should be your highest priority. The better the retrieval capability of the model, the more relevant code chunks it will retrieve when you ask a question about the codebase.

> 2. **Embedding Dimensions**: A higher embedding dimension may provide more nuanced representations of code and questions, improving retrieval accuracy. However, this needs to be balanced with memory usage and model size.

> 3. **Model Size (Million Parameters)**: Larger models tend to perform better in generating high-quality embeddings, but at the cost of memory and speed. Consider how much memory and computational power you can afford.

> 4. **Max Tokens**: A higher max token limit is useful for code because some functions or files can be quite large. You'll want a model that can handle bigger chunks of code.

> 5. **Classification Average (12 datasets)**: Code-related tasks sometimes involve classification (e.g., determining the type of question or identifying sections of code). A higher classification score can help in such scenarios.

> 6. **STS Average (10 datasets)**: Semantic Textual Similarity (STS) is also important as it measures how well the embeddings capture semantic meaning, which is useful for understanding the context and retrieving the right code section.

> ### Sorting Criteria:
> Sort by **Retrieval Average** first, then consider **Max Tokens** and **Embedding Dimensions** for practical handling of code and performance optimization.

> The other metrics like **Classification**, **Clustering**, and **Reranking** are less critical for your specific use case, but they can help refine the quality if you have a secondary need for such tasks.

title: `/start` git submodule
body:
Task matchmaking will be very powerful when coupled with the `/start` logic in this plugin.   

The key magic of `/start` is that it is designed to check if the assignee is already saturated with work (i.e. [^01^]         two concurrent tasks max) or needs to place their attention on any existing open pull reviews etc. 

Task recommendation should have the ability to automatically assign team members based on how saturated they are. If for example the top three recommendations are 

1. [^05^]      gentlementlegen 
2. whilefoo 
3.  shiv810 

But gentlementlegen is already working on two projects concurrently, then the matchmaking should automatically go to the next suitable candidate, Whilefoo. 

The purpose of this proposal is so that we don't need to duplicate the logic in that plugin for matchmaking. [^02^]   Instead, we should expose an API to have this plugin compute whether gentlementlegen can start or not.  

1. Let's clone the `command-start-stop` repository as a submodule inside of the `text-vector-embeddings` repository and then invoke the logic locally. 
2. Let's add the capability to assign collaborators based on the output of start logic. [^04^] 

### cron future 

We can constantly check the saturation state of team members and when they finish a project they can be automatically assigned to any that need to be completed, in order of priority. Then the AI manager will always be pushing to get urgent tasks done. 

It will also intelligently not assign those who are already disqualified because that's also in the start logic! [^03^]

title: Graph Compounding
body:
I'm focusing on the "breakeven line" idea and its ability to accurately express when a position is compounded (yield is withdrawn, and redeposited.) Instead of graphing it as a ray (a line with one "extended" point) we can instead graph it as a line segment (no extended points) from the position deposit time, to when a position's size is modified. 

If we add to the position size, then the breakeven line should be steeper (more fees should be accrued due to a larger position size.) I made a quick mockup using TradingView to illustrate this idea.

![ETHUSDT_2022-11-18_11-36-13](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4975670/202611303-0420fcc3-b379-46c0-a8d8-fba435562108.png)

title: Dynamic Data Input
body:
# Dynamic Data Input on TradingView Indicator

The first major engineering challenge in order for this to be useful in realtime decision making is to allow for the script to have the capability to dynamically read position data from external data sources. I did quite a bit of research on this, and it appears that TradingView's PineScript does not currently have this capability. 

Currently the variables must be manually input on the indicator. This can be done by double clicking the indicator on TradingView.

![tv](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4975670/201572439-f6443b3e-5f1c-4abc-bc15-e8a0be86a129.jpg)

Those variables are manually imported off of the data presented by Revert's UI

https://revert.finance/#/uniswap-position/mainnet/312741

The founder of Revert passed me along this draft documentation of their private API. This was current as of 12 July 2022, as such there may have been some changes introduced since. This will allow you to access the information in JSON format. 

[apidocs.md](https://github.com/ubiquity/uniswap-v3-liquidity-provider-indicator/files/9999366/apidocs.md)

---

# Research

I would encourage you to explore workarounds.

### Workaround Strategy A - API Reverse Engineering

I would highly recommend API reverse engineering so that there is FAR LESS infrastructure to support for this capability. Then we could have a super simple node program that simply pings Revert (GET) and TradingView (POST) APIs every minute or so.

### Workaround Strategy B - Scraper

Have a remote invoked [scraper](http://github.com/ubiquity/scraper) to manually log in to your TradingView account, and

1. pass in the updated parameters using the settings panel (this might be possible by directly making the API request to update the user's settings vs having to navigate through the UI) or 
2. to hardcode the parameters and update the PineScript code.

title: Deadlines Incentive
body:
After a few calls with prospective pilot partners, it’s clear that teams care about **accurate time estimates** and **incentivizing deadlines**. This command should make deadlines more useful while rewarding contributors fairly.  

---

## **Objective**  
Build the **command-deadline plugin** to:  
1. **Encourage accurate time estimates** by tracking task durations.  
2. **Reward early completion** while ensuring deadlines matter.  

---

## **How It Works**  

### **Setting a Deadline**  
- Use `/deadline [date]` to set a deadline on a GitHub issue.  
- The deadline is tied to UTC by default but can be configured.  
- If a GitHub Project View is linked, the GitHub issue should sync there on the timeline view (if configured).  

### **Rewarding Early Completion**  
By default, finishing a task **on time** earns **100% of the task value**. If completed **faster**, the contributor gets a **speed bonus**. If **late**, they either receive **a reduced payout or are disqualified** (configurable).  

---

## **Deadline Reward Ratio Configuration**  

Instead of always giving **double the task value** for instant completion, we introduce a **configurable coefficient**:  

- **`deadlineRewardRatio`** → Controls how much of the task value is used for speed bonuses.  
- Default is `0.25`, meaning a max bonus of **25% extra** for instant completion.  
- If set to `1.0`, instant completion can **double** the reward.  

For example, on a **$1600** task with `deadlineRewardRatio = 0.25`:  
- **Instant completion** → `$1600 + $400 bonus = $2000`  
- **Halfway to deadline** → `$1600 + $200 bonus = $1800`  
- **On deadline** → `$1600` (no bonus, no penalty)  
- **Past deadline** → Payout gradually decreases unless disqualification is enabled.  

---

## **Final Reward Formula**  

```text
reward = baseTaskValue * max(0, 1 + (deadlineRewardRatio * (1 - (actualCompletionTime / assignedDeadline))) - ((1 - disqualificationEnabled) * (actualCompletionTime / assignedDeadline)))
```

### **How It Works**  
- **If completed early**, the reward increases up to `1 + deadlineRewardRatio` times the base task value.  
- **If completed exactly on time**, the reward is **1× task value**.  
- **If late**:
  - If **disqualification is enabled**, the reward drops to **0** at `assignedDeadline`.  
  - If **disqualification is disabled**, the reward **gradually decreases** until **0 at `2 * assignedDeadline`**.  

---

## **Disqualification (Optional)**  
If enabled, contributors **must** complete the task before the deadline or they receive **nothing**. This can be toggled per project.  

---

### **Mock YAML Configuration File**
Here’s a **sample `config.yml`** with all possible settings and their **default values**:

```yaml
commandDeadline:
  timezone: "UTC"  # Default timezone for deadlines (UTC, PST, etc.)
  deadlineRewardRatio: 0.25  # Max speed bonus (0.25 = 25% extra, 1.0 = 100% extra)
  disqualificationEnabled: true  # Whether missing the deadline results in a 0 reward
  projectView: null  # Link to GitHub Project View if deadlines should sync there
```

### **Configurable Behavior**
- If `deadlineRewardRatio = 0.5`, contributors **can earn up to 50% extra** for early completion.  
- If `disqualificationEnabled = false`, contributors **still get some payout even if late**, but it gradually declines.  
- If `projectView` is set, deadlines will be **synced to the linked GitHub Project View**.  

---

### **Final Summary**
- `/deadline [date]` sets a deadline.  
- **Early = Bonus** (configurable via `deadlineRewardRatio`).  
- **On time = Full payout**.  
- **Late = Configurable penalty (default: disqualification, or gradual payout reduction if disabled)**.  
- **Deadlines can sync to GitHub Project Views** if linked.  

This setup keeps the system **flexible, fair, and easy to configure**.

title: New Task Or Edit Pull Arbiter
body:
### Overview

- During pull requests, we need to have a bias of always moving forward. 
- We always need to make an active decision on whether to include requested changes in the current pull or if we should break them off into new tasks. 
- We should use an LLM to help us decide what to do. 

### Ideal Outcome

We seamlessly can review pulls and then the OS can intelligently create a new issue batching the requested changes and link back. Ideal if it can batch similar tasks aiming for between 1 hour and 1 day within the same GitHub issue (I think this is possible with my experience working with Claude 3.5 Sonnet.)

### Implementation

The way that this can work is if we leave the review state as "commented" and leave comments that seem like we want changes to be done. These will need to be understood by the OS. 

If we really think the changes are critical to be done in the current pull, then we must be sure to "request changes." 

### Remarks

I am concerned that it might be too quick to fire off new issues, so ideally we have it understand that there is a possibly already created "post pull request review" task, and to continue batching everything from the review in that task (we could standardize the naming scheme of the generated issue.) 

We may possibly need to check for pull request closed and merged to process the full conversation and fire off new tasks.  This will allow us room to determine the necessity of such suggestions.

title: Developer Referrals
body:
In order to increase the size of our developer community, we should pay who refer developers who contribute. Let's call them DevRels.

The vision is to generate a new permit for the DevRel upon task completion. 
- It is unclear to me exactly how to do default pricing (flat fee, percentage) 
- It can be easy to game and squeeze out extra rewards with a self referral unless the DevRel's cut is zero sum from the developer. If it is zero sum, then a percentage would be safest i.e. 5% or 10%. What is the highest percentage we can go before developers get annoyed? Perhaps we can also set a max cap, like 50 USD per reward in addition to the percentage. For example, if a developer receives a 1000 USD reward, perhaps the DevRel cap can be max 50 USD. 
- I fear the rewards may not be very competitive. We can consider high (non zero sum) rewards in the short term to kick things off, then switch it to zero sum mode later.
- We should also consider a reward for every developer referred (this may be implemented as a separate tool though, because it seems incompatible with the percentage or zero sum system)

### Configuration Requirements

- For version one, lets go only with percentages support. Make sure that the percentage is configurable. 
   - This will keep things simple in case the DevRel earns a tiny reward for leaving a small comment etc.
- Make sure that we can toggle zero sum mode (subtract from developer reward to pay the DevRel.) 

### Implementation Overview

We can do this in a couple of parts:
1. the new referral logs in with GitHub at `work.ubq.fi?ref=[github user id of DevRel]` 
2. we associate in a KV store the key being the DevRel GitHub ID and the value being an array of DevRel values. 
```typescript
type GitHubId = number;
type KV = { [key: GitHubId]: GitHubId[] };
```

This feels a little parasitic if a developer accidentally clicks a DevRel's link at some point in the future, especially if they start losing some of their rewards. I think we need some type of system to "undo" a referral. This doesn't feel like the right solution but I'm unsure what is. 

### Sybils
We can protect against sybils based on the age of a GitHub profile as well as a minimum XP requirement. For example, if the developer XP is low then the rewards are zero sum?

title: refactor: init, utilities, and test envs
body:
During the initial build review everything was made to be `null` rather than optional which makes it a mess when initializing `rpc-handler` because everything must be explicitly set to `null` or `non-null`.

We should make all of the nullable init values optional to make it easier for the end user to set things up by removing the burden of having to consider if they actually need to define `runtimeRpcs` (which they don't) etc.

---

Maybe it would be helpful to build a utility class with the various helper functions already built and consider if there is a need for any others?

---

Since `pay.ubq.fi` tests has been a point of contention and it's likely to be used elsewhere too, we could build an `Anvil` module similar to the one used for testing there and beef it up to include more redundancies like caching the intended steps and if we fail kill and spawn another and rerun the script.

Idk if we can mess with `Anvil` at a low level, I know I can't, but other than that I don't see a clear solution to that problem from a `rpc-handler` perspective.

- dashboard specs below:

## Ubiquity OS — Frontend Dashboard Spec (GitHub UI Extension)

This spec focuses solely on the frontend dashboard interface for Ubiquity OS.  "XP" would simply replace the existing "WXDAI" units.

### Goal

Deliver a lightweight but powerful GitHub-native dashboard that makes developer contribution data visible, gamified, and actionable. The dashboard supports both developers and managers by surfacing metrics around impact, engagement, and performance.

### Dashboard Locations

1. **Organization-Level Dashboard** (`/orgs/[org]/xp-dashboard`)
2. **Repository-Level Dashboard** (`/repos/[org]/[repo]/xp-dashboard`)
3. **Contributor Profile Overlay** (opens on contributor click)

---

### Overview Panel

This is the main landing view for both org- and repo-level dashboards. It summarizes contribution activity over a selectable time range.

Includes:
- Total XP (timeframe + all-time)
- XP by category (Coding, Reviewing, Spec Writing, Comments)
- Leaderboard: top contributors by XP
- Velocity trend chart: XP earned over time
- Engagement overview: active contributors, PRs reviewed, comments made

### XP Categories

Each activity maps to one of several standardized XP categories:

| Category             | Description                                      | XP Source                                     |
|----------------------|--------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Spec Writing         | Issues authored with spec/design labels         | XP for creating and content quality           |
| Coding               | Merged PRs where user is assignee               | XP based on issue priority + estimated time   |
| Reviewing            | PR reviews, approvals, change requests          | XP per review and comment weight              |
| Commenting           | Comments on issues or PRs                       | XP for helpful or upvoted comments            |

### Contributor View

Drill-down modal or side panel for individual contributors.

Includes:
- Total XP (timeframe + all-time)
- XP by type (bar or donut chart)
- Weekly XP trendline
- Recent contributions (linked to GitHub)
- Most active repos or categories
- Optional badge/streak system for consistency

### Admin & Config Panel (Managers Only)

A separate settings interface available via external dashboard or modal:
- Adjust XP weight by category
- Configure labels for categories (e.g., what counts as "Spec")
- Set XP-to-bounty payout ratio (if applicable)
- Export XP data (CSV or JSON)
- Connect external bounty boards or task managers

### Repo-Level Analytics

When viewing a specific repository, the dashboard shows:
- XP earned within this repo
- Contributor leaderboard scoped to this repo
- Active XP categories in this repo
- Weekly velocity of contributions

### Design Considerations

- Fully styled to match GitHub’s native light/dark mode
- Minimal, readable typography using GitHub UI conventions
- Smooth animated transitions on XP updates and charts
- Color-coded tags and charts for fast visual scanning
- Real-time updates powered by GitHub webhooks

### Filters and Controls

- Date range selector (Last 7/30/90 days, custom)
- Repo selector (at org level)
- Sortable tables by XP, name, last activity
- Category toggles for filtering views
- Export data (button in bottom-right corner)
- "Open Full Dashboard" for expanded analytics (external app link)

---

Everything is configurable, so whether you need just performance analytics or a fully automated AI development manager, UbiquityOS adapts to fit your workflow—putting you in control every step of the way.

title: Base Pay Shift
body:
### Objective:
Analyze current compensation patterns and design an optimized incentive structure to reduce burn rate while ensuring high performers maintain or increase total compensation as base pay decreases.

### Requirements:

1. Data Analysis:
   - Track and analyze on-chain transactions for identified team wallets
   - Compare base pay vs incentive-based earnings for each team member
   - Calculate current burn rate and project future scenarios

2. Compensation Structure Design:
   - Plan quarterly base pay reduction steps (-$2k each)
   - Simulate incentive mechanisms (we have a tool for this) to project incentives based earnings. 
   - Target maximum 4 reduction phases from current max ($10k) to minimum/zero base pay

### Key Parameters:
- Maximum base pay: $10k
- Reduction increment: $2k per phase
- Implementation frequency: Quarterly maximum
- Total phases: Up to 4
- For this task only focus on the first reduction.

### Available Data:
- Team wallet addresses with associated identities
- Historical base pay records
- On-chain transaction history
- Performance metrics and incentive payments

### Deliverables:
1. Current state analysis of compensation distribution
2. Projected impact analysis of base pay reductions
3. Proposed incentive structure adjustments
4. Implementation timeline and phase triggers

### Success Criteria:
- Reduced overall burn rate
- High performers maintain or increase total compensation

Note: This is a one-time analysis project with specific implementation phases, not requiring ongoing maintenance.

title: Post Rick Onboarding Call
body:
This is a ChatGPT generated summary of the [meeting](https://fathom.video/calls/220187334?tab=summary). 

Will need to break down into separate issues. 

## Priority Shipping Based on the Conversation

1. Refocus on Core Value Proposition (High Priority)
	•	Shift focus from pricing/marketplace features to project management and efficiency tracking.
	•	Rebrand positioning to appeal to broader tech organizations beyond Web3.
	•	Develop a clear Unique Selling Proposition (USP) that differentiates from existing AI-powered project management tools.

2. Task Completion & Time Tracking (High Priority)
	•	Implement start and stop timestamps for task tracking:
	•	When an issue is assigned, record a timestamp.
	•	When a PR closes the issue, calculate total work time.
	•	Compare time estimates vs. actual completion time.
	•	Track developer work habits and efficiency over time.
	•	Generate reports for managers to see how accurately time estimates align with execution.

3. AI-Driven Task Recommendation System (High Priority)
	•	Improve vector embeddings to automatically assign relevant tasks based on historical work.
	•	Display task recommendations on GitHub issues with >80% relevance.
	•	Make task assignments more dynamic, allowing managers to see which developer is best suited for a task.

4. Automate Prioritization & Kanban Sorting (Medium Priority)
	•	Automatically sort tasks in GitHub project boards based on priority labels.
	•	Ensure high-priority issues don’t get buried in multiple pages.
	•	Provide dashboard insights showing what’s most urgent and who is assigned to what.

5. Launch Customer Discovery & Validation (High Priority)
	•	Conduct 10+ customer interviews in the next 2 weeks with tech orgs (outside Web3) to refine feature set.
	•	Ask: Would they pay for automated efficiency tracking?
	•	Identify common pain points in software project management that overlap across companies.

6. Create a New Landing Page & Demo (High Priority)
	•	Remove all crypto/Web3 references.
	•	Focus messaging on:
	•	How it helps project managers track efficiency.
	•	How it improves team prioritization and accountability.
	•	How it offers AI-driven insights into engineering workflows.
	•	Add a waitlist button and simple form:
	•	“Company Name”
	•	“Role”
	•	“Biggest challenge in managing development teams”
	•	Share the landing page with GitHub Universe contacts, OpenQ, and broader tech communities.

7. Remove Marketplace-Dependent Features & Test Core Plugins (Medium Priority)
	•	Set up mock repositories that exclude pricing/marketplace features.
	•	Test standalone plugins for efficiency tracking, prioritization, and task recommendations.
	•	Measure whether these features still deliver strong value.

8. Ensure Recommendations Work With Limited Data (Medium Priority)
	•	Improve logic so recommendations work even with a small number of completed issues.
	•	Allow recommendations based on job titles, GitHub contributions, and past assignments.

Next Steps
	1.	Set a 2-week goal to interview 10+ tech orgs and refine core features based on common feedback.
	2.	Launch the new landing page and waitlist ASAP.
	3.	Ship the task tracking & efficiency reports ASAP, as Riccardo expressed willingness to pay for this today.
	4.	Test AI task recommendations and priority automation, improving GitHub integration.
	5.	Remove crypto dependencies in key plugins and validate their standalone value.
	6.	Schedule follow-up call with OpenQ in a few weeks to show progress on the recommendations and efficiency tracking.

TL;DR – What to Prioritize?
	•	Efficiency tracking (time estimates vs. actuals).
	•	AI task recommendations (based on past work).
	•	Automatic prioritization & sorting (so high-priority tasks surface first).
	•	Customer interviews (to validate non-crypto use cases).
	•	Landing page & demo (to gather real-world interest).

This aligns directly with what Riccardo was most excited about and has the highest potential for market traction.

title: M12 Ventures for GitHub - Investee Relationship Building
body: ## Context > We’re delighted to highlight the first startup that has received our first investment out of the M12 GitHub Fund. Congratulations to [CodeSee](https://www.codesee.io/), an open source platform that enables developer teams to visually understand their codebases. Codesee is equipping developers with the best tools to unleash their capabilities and productivity and is shaping the way software developers work on their world-changing projects. This is central to why the M12 GitHub Fund was founded. [Source](https://m12.vc/news/announcing-the-m12-github-fund/). I looked up the founders, [CEO](https://www.linkedin.com/in/shaneak/), [CTO](https://www.linkedin.com/in/joshleven/) and I was inspired for a new idea: we should try and develop relationships with M12 investees. We can probably get introductions and advice through them. ## Investees > ByteWax > Python framework that enables you to build streaming data pipelines and real-time apps. > > > CodeSee > Cloud-based code visualization software that allows you to visualize, review, automate, and collaborate on your code. > > > Guardrails > Open source Python package for specifying structure and type and validating and correcting the outputs of large language models (LLMs). > > > Mermaid Chart > Text-based diagramming and charting tool that enables you to create and modify diagrams dynamically, built on top of the open source tool, Mermaid. > > > Nixtla > Open source library for building time series forecasting models and a proprietary time series LLM. > > > Novu > Open source notification infrastructure platform that provides a single API to manage all communication channels from one place. > > > Rubbrband > Provides a Python API to automatically evaluate AI-generated images from Stable Diffusion, SDXL, Midjourney, or DALLE-3. > > > OpenSauced > A platform that identifies the best developers and engineers by their code contributions and trending open source projects. > > > Tooljet > Low-code platform for building business applications using a drag-and-drop application builder. > > > Unify AI > Open source tool that unifies ML framework, infrastructure, hardware, and libraries to maximize the portability of ML codebases with one line of code. - [GitHub Source](https://resources.github.com/github-fund/). - [M12 Source](https://m12.vc/github-fund/). ## Additional Thoughts Slightly off topic but I'm more bullish on working with M12 because they seemingly recently added web3. Looks like we hit 66% of all their investment criteria. And then of course they made their dedicated GitHub fund which we also hit (so now 72%) its quite close to a perfect match. - [x] [AI](https://m12.vc/focus/ai/) - [ ] [Cloud infrastructure](https://m12.vc/focus/cloud-infrastructure/) - [ ] [Cybersecurity](https://m12.vc/focus/cybersecurity/) - [x] [Developer tools](https://m12.vc/focus/developer-tools/) - [x] [Vertical SaaS](https://m12.vc/focus/vertical-saas/) - [x] [Web3 + gaming](https://m12.vc/focus/web3-gaming/)[^1^] We could expect to get up to a $1M USD check from them, but unfortunately they don't generally lead rounds according to the GitHub source FAQ > Is the investment dilutive? > It is an equity investment. We typically do not lead investment rounds. For non-dilutive funding, please check out [GitHub Accelerator](https://accelerator.github.com/) or [GitHub Sponsors](https://github.com/sponsors). [^1^]: A funny name for an "AI + Blockchain" investee: https://fortune.com/crypto/2024/08/27/exclusive-microsoft-space-and-time-framework-lightspeed-arrington-ai-crypto/
